does: GrowLoop proposes a self-evolving conversation evaluation system that starts from minimal human seed annotations and iteratively extracts and refines evaluation rubrics through Heuristic Learning with LLM agents. The system continuously adapts evaluation criteria as language models advance and real-world scenarios shift. Rather than relying on static expert-authored benchmarks or fixed reward models, GrowLoop maintains an evolving rubric that captures implicit criteria of human-likeness in open-ended dialogue, dynamically adjusting these criteria as model capabilities improve and human expectations change. The approach addresses the core challenge that human-likeness is tacit knowledge that humans perceive intuitively yet cannot easily formalize, while recognizing that legitimate disagreement exists among human judges and that evaluation standards themselves are non-static.
connects: This work builds on prior research in LLM evaluation that has struggled with static evaluation methods, including expert-authored benchmarks that quickly become outdated, reward models that capture limited perspectives, and self-evolving benchmarks that lack human grounding. GrowLoop extends recent advances in self-improving evaluation systems by incorporating human feedback more directly into the rubric refinement process. It responds to the fundamental challenge identified across conversation evaluation literature that human judgments of quality are highly subjective, vary across annotators, and involve implicit criteria difficult to operationalize. The approach combines ideas from human-in-the-loop machine learning, where human annotations guide system development, with recursive rubric refinement strategies that allow evaluation criteria to evolve continuously rather than remaining fixed at initialization.
enables: GrowLoop enables more robust and adaptive evaluation of conversational AI systems that can keep pace with rapid model improvements without requiring constant expert annotation or benchmark redesign. By maintaining human-AI agreement as a core requirement while allowing rubrics to evolve, it provides a foundation for evaluation frameworks that capture emerging notions of human-likeness rather than lagging behind model capability growth. The system could support development of better conversation models by providing adaptive quality signals that adjust to shifting expectations. Future work could extend the approach to other open-ended generation tasks beyond conversation, explore how to balance human disagreement in rubric refinement, and investigate how evolved rubrics generalize across different conversation domains and user populations.
